explanation: There are two conditional statements: (1) If prop_0, then you prop_1, which translates to G (prop_0 -> prop_1), and (2) If prop_2, then you suddenly want to prop_3 and prop_4. 'Suddenly' is interpreted as 'next', meaning that in the next moment after prop_2 becomes true, prop_3 and prop_4 both become true. This is G (prop_2 -> X (prop_3 & prop_4)).

The specification states two separate rules: 1) If prop_0 holds, then prop_1 must also hold at the same time (i.e., always if prop_0 then prop_1). 2) If prop_2 holds, then both prop_3 and prop_4 must also hold at the same time (i.e., always if prop_2 then (prop_3 and prop_4)). Both are safety properties, to be written globally (G).

The statement asks for two implications: (1) If prop_0 holds, then prop_1 holds. This is written as G (prop_0 -> prop_1). (2) If prop_2 holds, then 'suddenly prop_3 and prop_4'—'suddenly' is commonly interpreted as 'next', meaning these must both be true in the next time step after prop_2. Thus, if prop_2 holds, then in the next state, prop_3 and prop_4 both hold. This is G (prop_2 -> X (prop_3 & prop_4)). The final LTL formula is the conjunction of these two properties.

The statement has two implications: First, 'If prop_0, prop_1' is a bit ambiguous, but it most likely means 'if prop_0 holds, then prop_1 must also hold' (i.e., globally, whenever prop_0 holds, prop_1 holds). Second, 'If prop_2, then prop_3 and prop_4' means that whenever prop_2 is true, both prop_3 and prop_4 must be true as well. Both are usually specified globally.

The statement lays out two conditionals: (1) 'If prop_0, prop_1' is interpreted as 'If prop_0 holds, then prop_1 must also hold (at the same time or immediately)', and (2) 'If prop_2, then you suddenly prop_3 and prop_4', meaning that if prop_2 becomes true, then immediately (in the next step) both prop_3 and prop_4 are true. The word 'suddenly' typically means next-step in LTL, so we use 'X'. These two conditionals need to hold globally.

The first part, 'If prop_0, you need prop_1', is interpreted as: whenever prop_0 holds, prop_1 must also hold in that state (globally (prop_0 -> prop_1)). The second part, 'If prop_2, then you suddenly want to prop_3 and prop_4', maps to: whenever prop_2 holds, then in the next state both prop_3 and prop_4 must hold (globally (prop_2 -> X (prop_3 & prop_4))). Both constraints are put together as a conjunction.

The natural language says: 'If prop_0, prop_1.' This implies: whenever prop_0 holds, prop_1 must also hold at that time (i.e., globally, whenever prop_0, prop_1). The second part says: 'If prop_2, then prop_3 and prop_4.' This means: globally, whenever prop_2 holds, both prop_3 and prop_4 must also hold at that time. Both conditions must be true globally, so we conjunct them.

The sentence states two requirements: (1) Whenever prop_0 holds, then prop_1 should also hold at the same time. (2) Whenever prop_2 becomes true, then immediately (i.e., in the same step) prop_3 and prop_4 both hold. The word 'suddenly' suggests that prop_3 and prop_4 should happen at the same time as prop_2 becomes true.

The first sentence, 'If prop_0, you prop_1,' is a global implication: whenever prop_0 holds, prop_1 must also hold. The second sentence, 'If prop_2, then you suddenly want to prop_3 and prop_4,' suggests an immediate requirement: as soon as prop_2 holds, both prop_3 and prop_4 must hold in the next state (i.e., suddenly = next).

The specification contains two main requirements: (1) If prop_0 holds at any point, then prop_1 must hold as well (an implication that is true globally); and (2) If prop_2 holds at any point, then immediately (in the next state, i.e., 'suddenly'), both prop_3 and prop_4 must hold. This is captured in LTL as (G (prop_0 -> prop_1)) for the first requirement, and (G (prop_2 -> X (prop_3 & prop_4))) for the second requirement. Both are required to hold globally, so combine with conjunction.
